topic: A person discovers their teenage diary has been published online
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eleanor stared at the screen, her breath catching in shallow, rapid gasps. The headline, splashed across the front page of a major lifestyle blog, featured a grainy, unflattering photo of her, paired with a snippet of text that was unmistakably from her private journal. A knot tightened in her chest, a cold, heavy sensation spreading through her limbs. She clicked the link repeatedly, her fingers trembling so badly the mouse danced erratically across the trackpad. "No," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet room. She slammed the laptop shut with a sharp crack that echoed the sudden collapse of her composure. She walked to the window, pressing her forehead against the cool glass, trying to physically rearrange the internal chaos churning within her. The sheer audacity of it made her skin crawl.

[story 2]

Mark sat hunched over the desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting in his wide, unblinking eyes. He reread the article three times, his jaw working silently. Each repetition brought a fresh wave of tightness behind his sternum. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal against the hardwood floor sounding loud in the apartment. He paced the small space, running a hand through his already disheveled hair. "This isn't fair," he muttered, his voice low and tight, laced with a brittle edge. He stopped abruptly, staring at the exposed words. A flush crept up his neck, hot and undeniable. He felt a desperate need to lash out, to smash something, anything, to regain a sense of control over the situation that had utterly slipped through his fingers.

[story 3]

Sarah slowly lowered the tablet, her hands resting flat on the desk, knuckles white. The words swam before her eyes, mocking her with their careless exposure. A strange, hot pressure built behind her eyes, making it hard to focus on anything else. She stood up, moving stiffly, as if her muscles were suddenly weighted with lead. She walked over to her bookshelf, pulling out a heavy textbook and setting it down with deliberate, forceful precision. Her breathing became shallow, quick little catches in her throat. She didn't want to look at the screen again; the sight of it felt like a physical assault on her sense of privacy, leaving her feeling utterly unsteady and deeply unsettled.
